Package com.saperion.common.lang.datatype

Class Decimal

java.lang.Object
java.lang.Number
com.saperion.common.lang.datatype.Decimal
All Implemented Interfaces:
Serializable, Comparable<Decimal>
public class Decimal extends Number implements Comparable<Decimal>

An immutable encapsulation class of signed decimal (fix-point) numbers. These numbers consist of an integral part and an optional fractional part.

This Decimal supports a maximum precision of 36. While ignoring leading zeros at the integral part, all other digits stated are considered relevant and count to the precision, thus f.e. 12340000; .00001234 and .00123400 all have a precision of 8.

This Decimal supports a scale between 0 and the actual precision (thus the maximum scale is 36 as well). Trailing zeros at the fractional part are considered relevant and count to the scale, thus f.e. .00123400 has a scale of 8.

Unlike BigDecimal, the equals(Object) method considers two Decimal objects equal if they are numerical equal (thus 2.0 is equal to 2.00) and compareTo(Decimal) and equals(Object) are consistent.

This Decimal does not support any arithmetic operations. Use the conversion to BigDecimal or double and vice versa and execute calculations with these objects. Take care when using double because it has a precision of about 17 decimal digits, so if the Decimal object to convert has a higher precision, the result is rounded to an approximated value.

There is no public constructor. Use one of the from...()-factory methods to create a Decimal.

The toString()-method returns a numerical representation of this decimal, that will be excepted by fromString(String) again.

An instance of this Decimal can be used in several threads concurrently.

Author:
agz

  • Method Details

    • fromString

      public static Decimal fromString(String string, int targetScale)
      Creates a new Decimal with the content of the specified String and the specified target scale.

      The string representation consists of an optional sign ('+' or '-'), followed by a sequence of zero or more decimal digits (the integral part), optionally followed by a fraction, optionally followed by an exponent.

      The fraction consists of a decimal point ('.') followed by zero or more decimal digits. The string must contain at least one digit in either the integer or the fraction. The number formed by the sign, the integer and the fraction is referred to as the significand.

      The exponent consists of the character 'e' or 'E' followed by an optional sign ('+' or '-') followed by one or more decimal digits.

      The 'natural' scale of the string will be the number of digits in the fraction, or zero if the string contains no decimal point, subject to adjustment for any exponent; if the string contains an exponent, the exponent is subtracted from the scale. If the scale becomes negative this way, it is zero instead.

      The specified scale may be -1 indicating that the natural scale should be used. Otherwise the specified scale must be greater or equal to the natural scale and the scale will be enlarged to the specified scale.

      The precision is the sum of the number of digits in the integral part and the effective scale. If the integral part is 0 it has 0 digits, thus a value of 0 with a scale of 0 has a precision of 0.

      Throws NumberFormatExceptions if an error parsing the string occurs or if the resulting value exceeds the capabilities of this Decimal.

      Parameters:
      string - string to parse
      targetScale - scale of the new Decimal or -1
      Returns:
      new Decimal
      See Also:
      • fromString(String)

    • fromString

      public static Decimal fromString(String string)
      Creates a new Decimal with the content of the specified String with the natural scale.

      See fromString(String, int) for further details.

      Parameters:
      string - string to parse
      Returns:
      new Decimal
      See Also:
      • fromString(String, int)

    • getScale

      public final int getScale()
      Returns the scale of this Decimal.
      Returns:
      the scale of this Decimal

    • enlargeScale

      public final Decimal enlargeScale(int targetScale)
      Creates and returns a new Decimal with the same numerical value but with the specified increased scale and an increased precision.

      The specified scale must be greater or equal to the current scale.

      As an increased scale leads to an increased precision, a NumberFormatException signaling a too large target precision may occur.

      Parameters:
      targetScale - scale of the new Decimal
      Returns:
      a new Decimal with the same numerical value and the specified scale

    • equals

      public boolean equals(Object obj)

      Unlike BigDecimal.equals(Object), this method considers two Decimal objects equal if they are numerical equal (thus 2.0 is equal to 2.00) and compareTo(Decimal) and equals(Object) are consistent.

      Overrides:
      equals in class Object
